title: Strategy Pattern
description: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms and let the client choose which one to use at runtime.

hook.crux: The Context stays. The Strategy swaps.
hook.oneLiner: Extract each algorithm into its own class, make them all implement the same interface, and let the context delegate to whichever one is plugged in.
hook.problemStatement: Imagine a checkout system that starts with credit card payments. Then PayPal is added, then UPI, then crypto. Each time, you crack open the same class and add another if-else branch. The class grows to 500+ lines. A junior dev accidentally breaks credit cards while adding crypto. Testing becomes a nightmare because everything is coupled together.

analogy.title: The Power Drill
analogy.description: A power drill (Context) stays the same forever. You never rebuild the drill. The drill bit (Strategy) is what you swap -- Phillips head for Phillips screws, flat head for flat screws, drill bit for making holes. Same drill, different bit, different result.

participants.name: Context
participants.role: The stable class that USES a strategy but doesn't know which one

participants.name: Strategy Interface
participants.role: The contract all algorithms must follow

participants.name: Client
participants.role: The code that decides which strategy to plug into the context

brainTriggers.description: When you hear these keywords in a problem statement, your brain should instantly light up with 'Strategy pattern!'

triggers.example: Multiple ways to calculate shipping cost
User can switch payment method during checkout
Pricing varies based on customer type
Discount policy changes for VIP vs regular customers

notStrategy.reason: That's event-driven notification, not algorithm swapping

notStrategy.keyword: object changes behavior based on internal state
notStrategy.reason: State transitions are self-driven, Strategy is client-driven

notStrategy.keyword: create object without knowing exact type
notStrategy.reason: That's about object creation, not algorithm delegation

brainTriggers.litmusTest: If the BEHAVIOR changes but the OBJECT using it stays the same, think Strategy.

steps.title: Define the Strategy Interface
steps.description: Create the contract -- one method that every algorithm must implement. Keep it focused: one interface, one job.

steps.title: Create Concrete Strategies
steps.description: Each algorithm gets its own class. Each class implements the interface. Each class owns ONLY its logic and data.

steps.title: Build the Context
steps.description: The Context holds a reference to the Strategy interface (not concrete class). It delegates work to the strategy. It NEVER knows which concrete strategy it holds.

steps.title: Client Wires It Up
steps.description: The client creates the concrete strategy and injects it into the context. The client is the ONLY one who knows which strategy to use.

java.title: Strategy Pattern - Payment System (Java)
java.explanation: Java uses interfaces as the Strategy contract. Each concrete strategy implements the interface. The Context holds a reference to the interface type, enabling runtime swapping. The client injects the desired strategy.

cpp.title: Strategy Pattern - Payment System (C++)
cpp.explanation: C++ uses abstract classes with pure virtual functions. Smart pointers (unique_ptr) handle memory via RAII. The 'override' keyword ensures compile-time safety.

kotlin.title: Strategy Pattern - Payment System (Kotlin)
kotlin.explanation: Kotlin combines concise syntax with strong safety. Constructor properties eliminate boilerplate, override is mandatory, lateinit handles deferred initialization, and lambdas can serve as lightweight strategies for simple cases.

solidConnections.connection: Add new strategies without modifying the Context. This is the CORE reason Strategy exists.
solidConnections.example: Adding CryptoPayment required ZERO changes to PaymentProcessor. We only ADDED a new class.

solidConnections.connection: Context depends on the Strategy interface (abstraction), not concrete classes.
Any concrete strategy is substitutable in the Context. Swap CreditCard for PayPal and Context works identically.
solidConnections.example: processor.setStrategy(new CreditCardPayment(...)) and processor.setStrategy(new PayPalPayment(...)) -- Context doesn't care which.

solidConnections.connection: Each strategy class has exactly one job -- its own algorithm. CreditCardPayment doesn't know about PayPal.
solidConnections.example: Validation logic for credit cards lives ONLY in CreditCardPayment, not spread across the Context.

lldProblems.where: Different payment processing algorithms (credit card, PayPal, UPI, crypto)
lldProblems.why: Payment methods are added/removed frequently. Each has completely different processing logic. Client (user) chooses at checkout.

lldProblems.where: Pricing calculation varies by vehicle type, time, and subscription
lldProblems.why: Parking lots constantly change pricing rules. Peak hours, weekends, subscriptions -- each is a separate algorithm. New pricing = new class.

lldProblems.where: Fare calculation differs by ride type
lldProblems.why: Fare algorithms change independently. Pool has shared-ride discounts, Premium has base fees. Surge wraps any fare with a multiplier (Strategy + Decorator).

lldProblems.where: Discount calculation varies by customer type and promotions
lldProblems.why: Promotions change weekly. VIP gets 20% off, employees get flat $50, festivals get BOGO. Each is a separate algorithm chosen at checkout.

lldProblems.where: Scheduling algorithm decides which elevator serves which request
lldProblems.why: Buildings have different needs. A hospital needs nearest-first. An office tower needs zone-based. A mall needs load-balanced. Same controller, different algorithm.

lldProblems.where: Eviction policy decides which entry to remove when cache is full
lldProblems.why: Different use cases need different eviction. Session cache needs LRU, CDN needs LFU, message queue needs FIFO. The cache itself doesn't change.

lldProblems.where: Delivery channel varies (email, SMS, push, Slack)
lldProblems.why: Users choose their preferred channel. New channels (WhatsApp, Teams) are added without touching existing ones. Client preference drives strategy selection.

lldProblems.where: Compression algorithm varies (ZIP, GZIP, RAR, LZ4)
lldProblems.why: Different files need different compression. Text compresses well with GZIP, images need LZ4 for speed. The compressor is the same, the algorithm swaps.

lldProblems.where: Auth method varies (password, OAuth, SSO, biometric)
lldProblems.why: Apps support multiple login methods. Enterprise adds SSO, mobile adds biometric. Each auth flow is completely different. Auth manager delegates to the strategy.

lldProblems.where: Sorting algorithm is swappable (bubble, merge, quick, heap)
lldProblems.why: The visualizer UI stays the same. User picks the algorithm. Each sort has different time complexity and behavior. Perfect Strategy use case -- the textbook example.

bestFriends.relationship: Creates the right strategy based on conditions. Instead of the client doing if-else to pick a strategy, the Factory encapsulates that logic.
Notifies listeners when strategy changes. Useful for logging, analytics, or UI updates when the algorithm switches.
bestFriends.example: When payment strategy changes, notify the analytics service

bestFriends.relationship: Wraps a strategy with extra behavior (logging, timing, caching) without modifying the strategy itself.
bestFriends.example: SurgeFare wraps any FareStrategy with a multiplier

evilTwin.similarity: Identical class structure -- Context holds interface, concrete classes implement it.
evilTwin.difference: In Strategy, the CLIENT chooses which algorithm to use. In State, the OBJECT ITSELF changes its behavior based on internal state transitions.
evilTwin.mnemonic: Strategy = YOU choose. State = IT changes itself.
evilTwin.note: Full comparison will be added after learning the State pattern.

evolution.direction: Strategy evolves into Template Method when algorithms share a common skeleton but differ in specific steps.
evolution.when: If 80% of your strategies share the same code, extract the skeleton into a base class.

antiPatterns.description: If the Context checks which concrete strategy it holds (instanceof, getClass, typeof), you've broken the pattern. The Context should be blissfully ignorant.
antiPatterns.goodCode:
// Move ALL logic into the strategy itself
strategy.pay(amount); // Context just delegates
antiPatterns.fix: Move the logic INTO the strategy. If the Context needs to behave differently per strategy, the strategy interface is too narrow -- add methods to it.

antiPatterns.trap: YAGNI -- Strategy when you don't need it
antiPatterns.description: Don't use Strategy when you only have ONE algorithm that won't change. Over-engineering with patterns is just as bad as under-engineering without them.
antiPatterns.goodCode:
// Just call the method directly
GzipCompressor.compress(data);
antiPatterns.fix: Ask: will this behavior ACTUALLY vary? If yes, Strategy. If not, just call the method. You can always refactor later.

antiPatterns.trap: Fat Strategy -- SRP violation inside strategies
antiPatterns.description: A strategy doing validation, logging, notifications, AND the algorithm violates SRP. Strategy does ONE thing -- the algorithm.
antiPatterns.fix: Strategy does the algorithm. Use Decorator for cross-cutting concerns (logging, timing). Use Observer for notifications. Use a separate Validator.

antiPatterns.description: Forgetting to set a strategy before calling the Context method causes a NullPointerException / segfault at runtime.
antiPatterns.fix: Initialize with a Null Object default strategy. Never leave the strategy field as null.

quiz.question: A class has a switch statement that decides whether to encrypt data using AES, RSA, or Blowfish. What pattern should you use to refactor this?
options: Strategy
Observer
Factory
Singleton
quiz.explanation: 'Different algorithms for encryption' + 'switchable' = Strategy. The EncryptionContext stays stable, the EncryptionStrategy swaps.

quiz.question: A developer's Context class has: if (strategy instanceof AESEncryption) { ... }. What's wrong?
options: instanceof in Context breaks the pattern -- Context should be ignorant of concrete types
Nothing wrong, instanceof is needed for type safety
Should use getClass() instead of instanceof
Strategy pattern doesn't support encryption
quiz.explanation: If the Context type-checks the strategy, you've broken the pattern. Move the differing logic INTO the strategy itself.

quiz.question: In a ride-sharing app, the user picks between Pool, Regular, and Premium rides. Who should be the CLIENT that chooses the strategy?
options: The controller/UI layer that handles user selection
The RideContext itself based on internal logic
The FareStrategy decides which type it is
A global configuration file
quiz.explanation: In Strategy, the CLIENT (controller/UI) decides which strategy to inject. The Context just delegates. If the object decides itself, that's the State pattern.

quiz.question: Which SOLID principle is the PRIMARY reason Strategy exists?
options: OCP -- extend behavior without modifying existing code
SRP -- each class has one responsibility
DIP -- depend on abstractions, not concretions
ISP -- don't force implementations of unused methods
quiz.explanation: OCP is the core driver. Strategy exists so you can ADD new algorithms (extend) without MODIFYING the Context (closed). DIP and LSP are important supporters, but OCP is the WHY.

quiz.question: Strategy and State have identical class structures. What's the KEY difference?
options: In Strategy the CLIENT chooses the algorithm; in State the OBJECT transitions itself
Strategy uses interfaces; State uses abstract classes
Strategy is behavioral; State is structural
There is no real difference
quiz.explanation: Strategy = YOU choose (client injects). State = IT changes itself (object transitions based on internal state). Same structure, different intent.

quiz.question: A payment strategy's pay() method validates the card, logs the transaction, processes the payment, and sends a receipt. What's the problem?
options: Fat Strategy -- violates SRP. Strategy should only process the payment. Use Decorator for logging, Observer for notifications.
Nothing wrong -- a strategy should be self-contained
Should use Template Method instead
The pay() method needs more parameters
quiz.explanation: A strategy should do ONE thing: its algorithm. Validation, logging, and notifications are cross-cutting concerns. Use Decorator for logging, Observer for notifications, and a separate Validator.

whenToUse: Multiple interchangeable algorithms for the same task
Behavior needs to be swappable at runtime
Giant if-else/switch deciding which behavior to execute
You want to add new behaviors without modifying existing code
whenNotToUse: Only one algorithm that won't change (YAGNI)
The behavior change is state-driven, not client-driven (use State)
The algorithms share 80%+ of their code (use Template Method)
You need to notify others about changes (use Observer)

structure.Context: Holds Strategy interface reference, delegates calls
structure.ConcreteStrategy: Each algorithm in its own class
structure.Client: Creates and injects the chosen strategy into Context

cheatSheet.sixWordSummary: The Context stays. The Strategy swaps.
cheatSheet.codeSmellTrigger: if-else / switch on type to decide behavior

practiceQuestions.title: Shipping Cost Calculator
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Strategy pattern for shipping cost calculation:
- ShippingStrategy interface with calculateCost(double weight, double distance)
- StandardShipping: $0.5 per kg per km
- ExpressShipping: $1.5 per kg per km + $10 flat fee
- SameDayShipping: $3.0 per kg per km + $25 flat fee
- ShippingContext that uses the strategy
- Demo swapping strategies at runtime
hints: Start with the interface -- one method: calculateCost(weight, distance)
Each concrete strategy implements its own pricing formula
The context just delegates: return strategy.calculateCost(weight, distance)

practiceQuestions.title: Ride-Sharing Fare Calculator (Strategy + Factory + Decorator)
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Strategy pattern for ride-sharing fare calculation:
- FareStrategy interface: calculateFare(double distance, int minutes)
- PoolFare: $0.5/km + $0.1/min
- RegularFare: $1.0/km + $0.2/min
- PremiumFare: $2.0/km + $0.5/min + $5 base
- SurgeFare: wraps any FareStrategy with a multiplier (Decorator)
- FareFactory: creates fare from ride type string
- RideContext with passenger info
hints: SurgeFare wraps another FareStrategy -- it's Strategy + Decorator
Factory takes 'POOL', 'REGULAR', 'PREMIUM' and returns the right strategy
RideContext stores passenger details and delegates fare calculation

practiceQuestions.title: Cache Eviction System
practiceQuestions.description:
Design a cache with pluggable eviction strategies:
- EvictionStrategy interface: selectEviction(metadata)
- LRUEviction: evict least recently used
- LFUEviction: evict least frequently used
- FIFOEviction: evict first inserted
- Cache class with get(), put(), configurable max size
- Use the eviction strategy when cache is full
hints: Cache tracks access time (LRU), frequency (LFU), and insertion order (FIFO)
The eviction strategy receives metadata and decides which key to evict
Use a map for storage and pass metadata to the strategy

bestPractices: Keep the Strategy interface focused -- one method for one algorithm
Use a Factory to create strategies when selection logic is complex
Always provide a Null Object default strategy to avoid null crashes
Never use instanceof or type-checking in the Context
Apply YAGNI -- only use Strategy when behavior actually varies
Each concrete strategy should follow SRP -- do one thing well
Prefer composition (Strategy) over inheritance for interchangeable algorithms
The Context should be blissfully ignorant of which concrete strategy it holds

commonMistakes.mistake: Using instanceof in the Context to check which strategy is set
commonMistakes.correct: Move logic into the strategy itself -- the Context just delegates

commonMistakes.mistake: Putting validation, logging, and notifications inside the strategy
commonMistakes.correct: Strategy does ONE thing. Use Decorator for cross-cutting concerns

commonMistakes.mistake: Creating Strategy when there's only one algorithm that won't change
commonMistakes.correct: Ask YAGNI: will this actually vary? If not, just call the method directly

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to set a strategy before calling the Context method
commonMistakes.correct: Initialize with a Null Object default: private Strategy s = new NoOpStrategy()
